data.title: The White House gunman was arrested; only nine public blockchains have earned over $100,000 in fees in the past seven days.
data.description:
Headline

▌Trump says gunman involved in White House Correspondents' Association dinner has been arrested

On April 26, US President Trump said on social media on the 25th that one of the gunmen involved in the "security incident" at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner had been arrested.

(Xinhua News Agency) According to Nansen data, only 9 public blockchains earned over $100,000 in fees in the past 7 days, with only 5 of them earning over $1 million. Ethereum topped the list with $10.81 million in fees, followed by Tron ($7.15 million), Solana ($3.43 million), BNB Chain ($2.48 million), Bitcoin ($1.42 million), Near ($858,500), Base ($723,500), Polygon ($448,900), and Arbitrum ($163,300). As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $78,214.63, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours; ETH price is $2,356.41, up 1.7% in the last 24 hours; BNB price is $641.52, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours; SOL price is $88.03, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours. DOGE price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +1.3%; XRP price is $1.45, 24-hour change +0.8%; TRX price is $0.3246, 24-hour change +0.5%; WLFI price is $0.07694, 24-hour change +2.1%; DOGE price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +1.3%; XRP price is $1.45, 24-hour change +0.8%; TRX price is $0.3246, 24-hour change +0.5%; WLFI price is $0.07694, 24-hour change +2.1%; WLFI price is $0.07694, 24-hour change +2.1%; XRP price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +1.3%; XRP price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +0.8 ... HYPE price is $42.37, a 24-hour change of +3.1%. Policy: Trump says he will not allow banks to undermine the cryptocurrency market. On April 26, CoinDesk, citing sources at a Trump cryptocurrency event, reported that Trump stated he would not allow banks to undermine the cryptocurrency market.

      Blockchain Applications

      ▌Litecoin Discloses Zero-Day Vulnerability Causing DoS Attack and Abnormal MWEB Transactions; Issue Now Fixed

      On April 26th, Litecoin disclosed on the X platform that a recent zero-day vulnerability had led to a DoS attack, affecting the operation of major mining pools. Mining nodes that had not updated in time allowed an invalid MWEB (MimbleWimble Extension Block) transaction to be executed, allowing the related tokens to be withdrawn to a third-party DEX. The Litecoin network rolled back these invalid transactions through a 13-block reorg, confirming that they would not be included in the main chain. During this period, all valid transactions were unaffected. The vulnerability has now been fully fixed, and the network has returned to normal operation.

        Cryptocurrency

        ▌Bitwise Submits Application for New Prediction Market ETF Related to Economic Recession and Tech Industry Layoffs on Friday Morning

        On April 26, according to Bloomberg ETF analyst James Seyffart on the X platform, Bitwise submitted an application for a new prediction market ETF related to economic recession and tech industry layoffs on Friday morning.

        ▌An entity transferred 34.61 million ASTER to Aster early this morning, worth $22.94 million

        On April 26, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin, an entity that withdrew 50.1 million ASTER from Aster last September transferred 34.61 million ASTER ($22.94 million) to Aster early this morning. ASTER subsequently dropped by 5% ($0.66 → $0.63). Currently, the entity's on-chain address still holds 24.25 million ASTER ($15.82 million).

        ▌An entity transferred 34.61 million ASTER to Aster early this morning, worth $22.94 million

        ▌Balancer attackers have exchanged 21,000 ETH for 617.43 BTC in the past three days

        On April 26, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, Balancer attackers have exchanged 21,000 ETH for 617.43 BTC in the past three days, worth $48.72 million. The attacker currently holds 1,000 ETH, worth $2.32 million, and may sell further.

        ▌RookieXBT linked wallet spends 25,000 USDC to buy 1.89 million SPIKE

        On April 26, according to Lookonchain monitoring, RookieXBT linked wallet spent 25,000 USDC to buy 1.89 million SPIKE.

        ▌20 million RAVE tokens were withdrawn from Bitget to 8 on-chain wallets by RAVE dealers 8 hours ago

        On April 26, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin, 8 hours ago, 20 million RAVE tokens (US$16.78 million) were withdrawn from Bitget to 8 on-chain wallets by RAVE dealers.

        Important Economic Developments

        ▌Trump: Never Considered Backing Down on Ceasefire

        On April 26, US President Trump stated: I have never considered backing down on the ceasefire issue.

        When asked about Iran's enriched uranium, Trump said, "They offered a lot of conditions, but it wasn't enough." He stated, "The whole agreement isn't complicated." Trump reiterated that Iran must never possess nuclear weapons. Disclosure documents show Trump purchased at least $51 million in bonds in March. According to financial disclosure documents released Saturday, April 26, US President Trump purchased at least $51 million in bonds across multiple industries in March. According to forms released by the US Government Ethics Office, Trump conducted 175 financial transactions last month. These forms did not list the specific amounts for each transaction, only providing the range. The disclosed assets were mostly municipal bonds issued by states, counties, school districts, and other entities affiliated with government agencies or public-private partnerships. His 26 largest transactions, ranging from $1 million to $5 million, were primarily municipal bonds or U.S. Treasury bonds, although two of them involved the purchase of corporate bonds from Wieselhaus and General Motors. He also invested in an exchange-traded fund that tracks a high-yield bond index. Trump purchased corporate bonds in the energy, technology, healthcare, and financial services sectors, with issuers including Constellation Energy, Occidental Petroleum, Broadcom, Nvidia, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, and Wall Street banks Citigroup, Goldman Sachs, and JPMorgan Chase, as well as Boeing. Trump's total bond purchases across all asset classes amounted to approximately $161 million.

        ▌Iranian Foreign Minister to Visit Pakistan Again

        On April 26, the Islamic Republic News Agency (IRNA) reported on the 25th that, according to a plan released by the Iranian Foreign Ministry, Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi will visit Pakistan again after concluding his visit to Oman and before heading to Russia.

        (Xinhua News Agency) On April 26, according to Iranian sources, Iranian Deputy Speaker of the Islamic Republic of Iran, Nikozadeh, stated that Iran's Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, has explicitly ordered that the Strait of Hormuz must not return to its pre-war state. (CCTV News) Golden Encyclopedia Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" Amid Geopolitical Crises? Every time a geopolitical crisis occurs, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been substantiated by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Referral rewards in cryptocurrency projects do not equate to pyramid schemes or other crimes: it depends on where the money comes from.
data.description:
If a cryptocurrency project possesses specific characteristics, combined with the current domestic policy of severely cracking down on cryptocurrency speculation, there is a significant criminal risk of it being classified as the crime of organizing or leading pyramid schemes.

In the previous article "Caught in a Cryptocurrency Pyramid Scheme, the Involved Projects Are Usually These Four Types, Attorney Shao has already outlined the typical models and variations of cryptocurrency projects involved in pyramid schemes.

However, the concept of Web3 iterates rapidly, and models emerge endlessly. In judicial practice, there are also instances where judicial organs, due to their unfamiliarity with Web3 project models, have mistakenly judged some Web3 projects with genuine business logic as pyramid scheme crimes.

This article aims to explore: What kind of Web3 project models should not be considered pyramid scheme crimes? Where exactly is the lawyer's room for defense? This article will analyze this issue using specific cases. Let's look at a case first. Machael and others built a cryptocurrency platform and issued cryptocurrency X. The project model is as follows: For every transaction of X tokens by a downline member, the platform collects a certain percentage of the service fee, and at the same time, 20% of the service fee is awarded to the upline who developed that downline member as a referral reward. In this case, do Machael and others constitute the crime of organizing and leading a pyramid scheme? From a formal perspective, the platform pays referral rewards to its upline based on the transactions of its downline, which seems to meet the formal requirement of "using the number of people recruited as the basis for remuneration," and there seems to be little controversy in determining that it constitutes a pyramid scheme crime? However, this judgment is clearly too hasty. Distinguishing between pyramid scheme crimes and administrative violations of team-based remuneration: The key is to look at the source of the upline's profits. According to the 2013 "Opinions of the Supreme People's Court, the Supreme People's Procuratorate, and the Ministry of Public Security on Several Issues Concerning the Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Organizing and Leading Pyramid Schemes," simple "team-based remuneration" pyramid schemes that aim to sell goods and use sales performance as the basis for remuneration are not treated as crimes. This regulation establishes the core distinction between pyramid scheme crimes and administrative violations in practice: What is the source of the upline's profits—the principal of the downline or the platform's actual operating income? If it comes from the downline's principal—essentially "robbing Peter to pay Paul," using money from later participants to pay for earlier ones' profits—it's a typical Ponzi scheme and constitutes a suspected pyramid scheme crime. However, if it comes from profits generated by the platform through genuine business operations—then it can be argued that the team-based compensation model is an administrative violation, not a crime. Therefore, the starting point for lawyers handling such cases is to determine the source and nature of the funds for upline rewards in the specific model of the project in question. Cryptocurrency projects involved in pyramid schemes: How to determine if a platform has genuine operating income? Returning to Machael's case at the beginning. To prove that the platform does not constitute a pyramid scheme, it is necessary to combine the specific circumstances of the project and find a plausible business logic—proving that the platform's reward funds for upline members are earned by itself, not stolen from downline members. This needs to be considered in two scenarios. Test 1: Besides reselling, what else can tokens be used for? If the sole purpose of the token within the platform is to resell it to the next participant, or to exchange it for USDT in a self-built "fake DEX" (where USDT is the initial investment of later entrants), then the token has no independent use value, the project has no real operating income, and the funds for launch rewards can only come from the initial investment of new users, making a Ponzi scheme undeniable. Conversely, if the token can be used to purchase goods or services with independent value within the application—NFT equipment, membership benefits, data services, game items, etc.—and the consumption proceeds can be traced back to the project's treasury, then the project has a factual basis to claim "real operating income." Test Two: Can you participate without buying the token? This is the key to determining whether purchasing cryptocurrency constitutes an "entry fee" for a pyramid scheme. On Web3 platforms, users often need to first exchange RMB for USDT, and then for the platform's token. Whether this cryptocurrency purchase qualifies as an entry fee depends on this: If, without purchasing the token, an account cannot be activated and a referral link cannot be generated, the purchase is forcibly linked to participation eligibility, thus risking being considered an entry fee. Conversely, if users can register for free and obtain initial tokens by completing tasks, but earning tokens is slower without purchasing them, the mandatory nature of the purchase is insufficient, and it should not be considered an entry fee. Taking Move-to-Earn projects as an example: Three qualitative results. Taking "Run-to-Earn" projects as an example, combining the two testing criteria mentioned above, the same project type may fall into three completely different categories in terms of legal nature. Tier 1: Not Constitutes Any Pyramid Scheme or Illegal Activities Users can use basic functions for free; purchasing NFT running shoes is merely an added-value option, not a mandatory entry barrier; referral rewards are based on the actual spending amount of the referred user (such as NFT royalty sharing), not the number of people recruited; tokens can be used for in-app purchases, buying items, and paying for services, demonstrating real-world usage scenarios; the project team has real operating income such as NFT royalties and advertising partnerships, and the launch rewards originate from this, not from new users' initial investment. —This model has neither a mandatory entry fee nor a tiered rebate structure, and therefore does not constitute any form of pyramid scheme or illegal activity. Second Tier: Constitutes a Team-Based Compensation-Based Administrative Violation, But Not a Crime. There is a tiered rebate structure where uplines can receive rewards from the consumption and purchasing behavior of downlines; however, rebates are calculated based on the sales performance (amount or quantity of NFTs purchased) of downlines, not on the number of people recruited; the project aims to sell NFTs/tokens, and there is a genuine flow of goods; there is no subjective intent to defraud. — This meets Article 7, Paragraph (3) of the "Regulations on Prohibiting Pyramid Schemes," and is a team-based compensation-based pyramid scheme, but not a crime. The third tier: Constitutes a pyramid scheme crime. This involves forcing participants to purchase expensive NFTs/tokens as an entry threshold; calculating rebates directly based on the number of people recruited, regardless of actual consumption; promising high-interest static returns, with funds originating from the principal of later recruits; tokens having no real consumption scenario, serving only as a pyramid scheme accounting tool; and having the subjective intent to defraud. —Simultaneously meeting the four elements of "entry fee + tiered structure + recruitment-based compensation + defrauding," this constitutes the crime of organizing and leading a pyramid scheme. One additional point: What does it mean if the project lacks a real consumption scenario? This is precisely the crux of most cryptocurrency pyramid schemes. If the platform's tokens, aside from being resold within the platform to the next participant, have no real consumption scenarios—if users buy tokens solely to wait for appreciation or obtain static returns—then the project lacks any real operating income, and the funds for referral rewards can only come from the principal of new users. Regardless of how the platform's internal reward rules are designed, the underlying structure of the entire model is a Ponzi scheme of "robbing Peter to pay Paul," making it difficult to change the fact that the project itself is a pyramid scheme. Defense Points: The following four points must be verifiable simultaneously. If the project team intends to claim that it does not constitute a pyramid scheme crime, or only an administrative violation, then the following four points must be verifiable simultaneously: The tokens have real consumption scenarios and can be used to purchase goods or services with independent value within the application; The consumption funds do indeed enter the project team's treasury, and the on-chain fund flow is traceable; The referral rewards originate from the project team's revenue, rather than being directly deducted from the principal of the downline; The reward is triggered upon completion of consumption, not upon purchase or staking of tokens. If evidence is missing or broken in any of the above stages, the risk of classifying it as a pyramid scheme crime will significantly increase. In conclusion, such cases involve the project's token economic design, on-chain fund flows, and verification of the authenticity of consumption scenarios. If judicial authorities are unfamiliar with Web3 business models, it can lead to deviations in the classification of related cases. Furthermore, the Web3 field itself iterates extremely rapidly; when each new model emerges, the corresponding judicial understanding is often still lacking. However, this also means that there is considerable room for defense in these types of cases. As a defense attorney, one needs to be familiar with the business model and operational logic of such projects to find an effective entry point.

data.title: ETFs see net inflows for six consecutive days: Is Bitcoin about to enter an $80,000 short squeeze?
data.description:
Author: Ma He, Foresight News

On April 23, Bitcoin was priced around $78,000, having broken through $79,000 yesterday, reaching a new high since February this year. ETH is still fluctuating around $2,400, while some altcoins have seen significant gains.

According to Coinglass data, in the past 24 hours, the total open interest liquidation data for contracts across the network reached $462 million, with short positions liquidated at $353 million. According to the latest data from CMC, the market fear and greed index has rebounded to 60, with sentiment remaining neutral.

Global risk assets continued their rebound momentum.

Global risk assets continued their rebound momentum.

... U.S. stocks hit new record highs again yesterday: the S&P 500 closed at 7,137.90 points, up 1.05% on the day; the Nasdaq Composite Index closed at 24,657.57 points, up 1.64%, a new record high; and the Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at 49,490.03 points, up 340.65 points or 0.69%. Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar index (DXY) stabilized at a high level around 98.61, fluctuating slightly from the previous day. Trump announced on Wednesday an extension of the ceasefire agreement. Coupled with strong first-quarter corporate earnings reports, U.S. stocks quickly recovered from the previous two days of consecutive corrections. On Wednesday, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite closed at record highs, with chip stocks rising for the 16th consecutive day, while oil prices surged in tandem, diverging from market optimism. Trump announced an extension of the ceasefire at the request of Pakistani mediators, stating that US-Iran peace talks could resume as early as Friday, but Iran subsequently denied the possibility of Friday negotiations. The Iranian president stated that he "welcomes dialogue and agreements," but criticized Trump for "contradictions in his words and actions." Iranian Parliament Speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf stated that a comprehensive ceasefire is impossible if the blockade is not lifted. Oil prices surged on Wednesday, marking their third consecutive day of gains. WTI crude futures rebounded to levels seen before the breakdown of negotiations, while Brent crude approached $102. U.S. stocks closed higher on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 hitting a record high and the Nasdaq hitting a new all-time high, led by technology stocks. The S&P 500 technology sector rose about 2%, the best performing of the 11 sectors. Furthermore, according to the latest data from Polymarket, the market's probability of one Fed rate cut this year has risen to 30%. With expectations of easing tensions in the Middle East, the safe-haven appeal of the dollar has significantly weakened. Coupled with the return of market bets on a Fed rate cut this year, global funds are accelerating their withdrawal from dollar assets. Data shows that the US dollar index has fallen by about 2.3% from its high at the end of March, potentially marking its worst monthly performance since August last year. Wall Street institutions generally believe that this round of dollar weakness is essentially driven by a dual factor: the fading of safe-haven premiums and a shift in policy expectations. JPMorgan Chase has restarted its short-dollar strategy and turned bullish on risk currencies such as the Australian dollar; Bank of New York Mellon also pointed out that emerging market currencies have rebounded across the board, reflecting a significant recovery in global risk appetite. BTC spot ETFs have achieved net inflows for six consecutive days. From April 14th to 21st, the total daily net inflow into the ETF was positive for all six days.

Among them, the net inflow on April 17th reached a high of $663.91 million, the highest in recent times; April 14th and April 20th recorded net inflows of $411.5 million and $238.37 million respectively; April 15th contributed $186 million.

On the other hand, there were only 4 days of net outflows, and the outflow amount did not exceed $400 million on any single day.

As for Ethereum spot ETFs, since April 9th ​​of this year, they have achieved a rare 9 consecutive days of net inflows. On April 17th, a net inflow of 127 million was recorded in a single day, setting a new high for the month. Net outflows only occurred on four days this month. Regarding stablecoin data, according to DefiLlama, it has now risen to $320.6 billion, with a net inflow of $635 million over the past seven days. Subsequent Trends: Glassnode released a data chart stating that Bitcoin has recovered the $78,000 mark, with both spot demand and ETF inflows returning. Short positions, coupled with the accumulation of negative funding rates, create the potential for a short squeeze. However, excessively high realized profits and low volatility are sending cautionary signals, and upside potential around $80,000 faces resistance. Bitcoin tweeted that new sources of Bitcoin demand are gradually emerging. Strategy's continued buying provides relatively stable buying support for the market, and clearer signs of allocation-oriented fund inflows are beginning to appear. Specifically, Coinbase Premium continues to rebound, with the spot Bitcoin ETF seeing a net inflow of approximately $664 million in a single day, the highest level since mid-January. These signals all point in one direction: the demand structure is recovering. Corporate financial buying, ETF inflows, and US spot demand are working together to solidify support at price lows, and market participation is also rebounding, a significant departure from the environment of the previous correction phase. Combined with yesterday's analysis of stablecoin fund inflows, liquidity support is also gradually strengthening. These two lines of reasoning suggest the market may be gradually building a new trading range. This doesn't mean the price will rise linearly, but if the above trends continue, the probability of prices moving towards the upper limit of the range is increasing.

data.title: a16z Crypto: 9 Charts to Understand the Evolution of Stablecoins
data.description:
Author: a16z crypto; Translator: Jiahuan, ChainCatcher

Stablecoins have been searching for their place for years.

Initially, they were simply a trading instrument, a means of transferring dollars across exchanges. They then evolved into a savings instrument, held rather than consumed. Now, data points to a new direction: stablecoins are gradually becoming core financial infrastructure.

The following nine charts reveal the factors driving this trend.

Regulation Accelerated Market Growth

For much of the stablecoin development process, regulatory uncertainty limited institutional participation. Later, the GENIUS Act brought regulatory clarity. It didn't create the trend, but it did amplify it.

In the United States, the GENIUS Act established the first federal-level framework for stablecoin issuance. This shift is clearly visible in the data: before the act's passage, adjusted trading volumes had been rising for several consecutive quarters, and the growth accelerated further after its passage, reaching approximately $4.5 trillion in the first quarter of 2026.

European regulatory frameworks—the Crypto Asset Markets Act (MiCA)—tell a more complex story. When the act fully came into effect at the end of 2024, several major exchanges delisted USDT for compliance, leading to a surge in activity in non-USD stablecoins, exceeding $40 billion at one point.

Subsequently, trading volume stabilized above pre-MiCA benchmark levels, at approximately $15 billion to $25 billion per month. Regulation created a persistent market for non-USD stablecoins that was virtually nonexistent before.

Stablecoin business activity is growing

Perhaps the most noteworthy structural change lies in how people actually use stablecoins.

Stablecoin business activity is growing

The most noteworthy structural change may lie in how people actually use stablecoins.

In terms of raw transaction volume, the C2C category far surpasses all other categories: reaching 789.5 million transactions in 2025. However, consumer-to-business stablecoin transactions saw the fastest growth, reaching 284.6 million transactions in 2025, more than doubling (+128%) from 124.9 million in 2024. Data from stablecoin card infrastructure highlights this trend. The monthly collateral deposits for stablecoin card projects backed by Rain (including Etherfi Cash, Kast, Wallbit, etc.) grew from near zero in November 2024 to over $300 million per month by early 2026. While this is collateral supporting consumption, rather than direct consumption of stablecoins, the trajectory is remarkable: stablecoin commercial activity is on the rise. Stablecoin velocity of circulation is accelerating. The turnover rate of each dollar of stablecoin supply is increasing. Since the beginning of 2024, the velocity of stablecoins (the adjusted ratio of monthly transaction volume to circulating supply) has nearly doubled, climbing from 2.6x to 6x. This increased velocity of circulation means that demand for stablecoin transactions exceeds new issuance, and the existing supply is working harder to keep up with demand. This is a hallmark of a true payment network—the base money is being actually used, not just held. Stablecoin trading volume reflects more payment activity. If we exclude transactions, fund flows, and exchange mechanisms, which account for the bulk of stablecoin trading, the total amount of payments between different entities last year is still estimated at $350 billion to $550 billion. In terms of transaction volume, business-to-business (B2B) transactions dominate stablecoin payments (which is unsurprising given their size). However, other areas, such as direct consumer-to-consumer payments and transactions with merchants, are also expanding rapidly. Stablecoin payments are currently concentrated in specific regions. Geographically, stablecoin payment activity is unevenly distributed. Nearly two-thirds of the transaction volume comes from Asia, mainly concentrated in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Japan. North America accounts for about a quarter. Europe accounts for about 13%. Latin America and Africa combined account for only a very small portion, less than $1 billion. Beyond cross-border payments, it's about local currencies running on a global track. The development of non-USD stablecoins is not limited to Europe; it is also emerging in emerging markets, driven by various factors. Brazil is a prime example. BRLA (a stablecoin backed by the Brazilian real) saw its monthly transaction volume grow from near zero in early 2023 to approximately $400 million per month in early 2026. Access to the PIX Instant Payments Network fueled its adoption. While stablecoins are often described as cross-border tools, the proportion of cross-border activity has actually been declining, not increasing. Domestic transactions (stablecoin transfers within the same country) have grown from about half of total payments in early 2024 to nearly three-quarters in early 2026. What does this mean? Stablecoins have not only established themselves as remittance or foreign exchange tools, but have also become a local payment medium operating on a global infrastructure. Taking all these factors together, a clear picture emerges, though not as most people expected: many once thought stablecoins would focus entirely on cross-border transactions. Instead, they are becoming increasingly localized. While the US dollar remains the core fiat currency pegged to the vast majority of stablecoins, stablecoins are by no means simply an export of the US dollar. Non-dollar variants, such as euro-backed and Brazilian real-backed local currency stablecoins, are gaining popularity. While peer-to-peer (C2C) stablecoin transfers far outnumber other types of payment flows, an increasing number of use cases are shifting towards everyday consumer spending (C2B). Quarterly data provides further evidence that stablecoins are evolving into a universal payment infrastructure. They are designed for global reach but are becoming increasingly localized in practice. It is still in its early stages, but the form of this system is gradually becoming clearer.

data.title: After reporting on AI for several years, I increasingly feel like a fraud.
data.description:
Foreword:

This is insane! This is probably the most intensive month of updates we've seen since APPSO started reporting on AI.

After introducing a host of new models, today's article wants to talk to you about the "surreal" phenomenon arising from the release cycle of the AI ​​industry. In the ever-evolving cycle of AI development, AI media outlets, including APPSO itself, have become "accomplices." Ultimately, everyone in their position has a role to play, and the direction of the tide is difficult to resist. However, we've noticed that more and more people share similar sentiments, so this article serves as a form of self-reflection. It all started with Opus 4.7. Looking only at the scores of most individual benchmark tests, you might think that Anthropic has once again raised the ceiling for large models. However, after its release, users who actually used Opus 4.7 did not feel the same way. Token consumption became even more outrageous than its predecessor, and contextual capabilities and tool invocation capabilities in coding scenarios both regressed. After using it for a while, some developers switched back to Opus 4.6. Gergely Orosz, author of Pragmatic Engineer, said that this new model "feels like it's working against me." "Opus 4.7 is a serious regression, not an upgrade." Many developers expressed similar views. Business Insider reported that tweets criticizing Opus 4.7 received tens of thousands of likes. The benchmark test results describe Opus 4.7; user experience also describes Opus 4.7. However, the two are vastly different. This distinction points to a cultural phenomenon pervading the entire AI industry: the AI ​​industry, especially companies like Anthropic and OpenAI, strives to maintain a model/AI product launch cycle, releasing major versions every few months or even weeks, with new features introduced daily. However, this relentless launch cycle, like stepping on one foot while the other steps on the other, has become a self-sustaining new economic model. The act of "releasing" itself, along with the marketing materials and media reports surrounding these releases, is enough to shape and solidify people's perception of AI progress, and trigger real financial consequences (fluctuations in the stock prices of related companies). In other words, the cognitive progress of AI has preceded, and even largely replaced, progress in actual experience. Releases replaced experiences, and hype became reality. Baudrillard and the Surreal World of AI Before the main characters of *The Matrix* joined the cast and filming began, the Wachowski sisters, the creators, forced them to read Jean Baudrillard's *Simulacres et Simulation*. Cultural critics generally believe that *The Matrix* is a superb interpretation of Baudrillard's thought framework in popular culture: most people accept the fictional illusion, while only a few remain "realistically conscious." In the character of Cypher, you see that some people prefer a beautiful illusion to a harsh reality. The protagonists of *The Matrix* trilogy, however, choose the red pill—no matter how slim the possibility of defeating the Matrix, they must return to and regain control of their reality, and take control of their own subjectivity. Ironically, in 2003, the French magazine *Le Nouvel Observateur* interviewed Baudrillard about his opinion on *The Matrix*, and he said: *The Matrix* is absolutely the kind of film about the Matrix that the Matrix itself would produce. To translate Baudrillard's statement, he was actually criticizing *The Matrix* for falling into the popular cultural cliché of "defeating a powerful enemy and regaining self-control." He didn't believe *The Matrix* grasped the essence of the ideas it presented. On the contrary, Baudrillard argues that the distorting force of "phenomena" is so strong that there is no substantial difference between phenomena and reality—phenomena have long since replaced reality. A restaurant's menu becomes a concrete representation of the restaurant, yet you cannot taste the restaurant's flavor solely from the menu; people form their understanding of the size of different countries' territories from a flat world map, but when the two-dimensional scale is switched to the real world, it is inaccurate, even wildly inaccurate. Reality certainly exists, but in an era where phenomena precede reality, it has gradually become insignificant. Returning to the main topic of this article: In the release cycle of AI models and products, you'll find that everything is unfolding exactly as Baudrillard predicted in the "hyperreality." The supporting systems surrounding the latest AI models have developed so rapidly that they have become incredibly vast and complex, possessing a powerful and unshakeable self-sustaining ability. To the point that press releases, benchmark scores, media reports, etc., have become the "periphery" of AI itself, capable of shaping people's perception of AI; the user's actual experience with the model/product has become a negligible secondary factor. Today, the release of new AI products doesn't truly reveal the product. Rather, the act of releasing itself shapes a narrative, creating a phenomenon. And these narratives and phenomena replace the lived experience. The release itself is the "product."

Opus 4.7, Gemini, Sora

In the same week that Opus 4.7 was released, Figma's stock price plummeted three times.

In reverse chronological order: the last one was the Claude Design release; the middle one was the Opus 4.7 release; and the first one was on April 14th, two days before the Opus 4.7 release. On that day, The Information exclusively reported that Anthropic was preparing to release the Opus 4.7 model and design tools that week. On April 14th, it wasn't just Figma whose stock price plummeted: Adobe, Wix, GoDaddy, and many other companies also suffered losses—billions of dollars in market value evaporated, simply because a reputable media outlet reported on a product that didn't actually exist and couldn't be truly perceived by the outside world. If the current AI industry has completely degenerated into a "surreal" phenomenon where phenomena outweigh reality, then the stock sell-off panic triggered by The Information's report is a perfect example. Subsequently, Opus 4.7 was officially released. Anthropic claimed it was the most powerful Opus model ever, providing a series of benchmark scores to support its claim: SWE-bench Verified improved from 80.8% to 87.6%, and CursorBench from 58% to 70%. At the moment of its release, people were incredibly excited because this most powerful Opus model ever not only seemed like an undisputed improvement, but also offered more for the same price: $5/25 per million input/output tokens. After a while, a flood of real-world user experiences came in, and everything changed. Users discovered that Opus 4.7 significantly "degraded" its performance on many specific tasks. For example, developer and blogger Theo Browne found that even in Anthropic's official Claude desktop client, using Anthropic's official harness to orchestrate code-based tasks on Opus 4.7 still behaves poorly, such as failing to find the latest version of Node.js and ignoring official system prompts. Furthermore, Opus 4.7's score in the Multi-Turn Context Retrieval (MCMR) benchmark is less than half that of its predecessor, Opus 4.6. This benchmark's focus on context retrieval capabilities could have a significant impact on Anthropic's core industry users, including those in law and finance, who genuinely need to work with extremely long context windows (millions of frames). Boris Cherny, the lead creator of Claude Code, personally declared that MCMR is a terrible, outdated benchmark, and that GraphWalk will be used as the benchmark for contextual testing in the future. Cherny's explanation is not very convincing. He says that MCMR has no practical significance, but one could also say that GraphWalk, a test that evaluates graph traversal performance using hexadecimal hash values, is no different from MCMR; both are meaningless and are "needle-in-the-sea" testing tasks. The new word segmenter used in Opus 4.7 also brings new negative performance improvements, potentially leading to additional processing time before input and output, with improvements of up to 35%. Within a day of the new model's release, some enterprise users reported that using Opus 4.7 on Claude for the same tasks previously performed by Opus 4.7 resulted in reaching the 5-hour limit 30%-80% faster than with version 4.6. Developer Abhishek Ray conducted in-depth testing of the new tokenizer in Opus 4.7 and found that the cost of reading documents (such as CLAUDE.md) is 1.45-1.47 times that of 4.6. Anthropic's official statement of a 0-35% token increase might lead you to believe that real-world scenarios will fluctuate within this range—however, in reality, the additional cost is even higher than Anthropic's "upper limit." What was Anthropic's response? Did they send Boris Cherney to X to verbally announce: "We've increased the spending limits for all users!"? When facts and narrative don't align, Anthropic ignores the facts and alters the narrative. This is like a feudal lord who loses territory in war; instead of trying to reclaim it, he changes the map and tells you, "My kingdom has always been this big." On the official benchmark table for Opus 4.7, there is another line of numbers belonging to the Mythos model: SWE-Bench Verified up to 93.9%, GPQA Diamond up to 94.6%, CyberGYM up to 83.1%—surpassing Opus 4.7 in almost every aspect. Mythos was "released" before Opus 4.7, but this "release" was far more bizarre than any Anthropic release ever: Ordinary users couldn't use Mythos on the day of its release. Anthropic merely announced the existence of this unprecedentedly powerful model, and only a select 50 partner companies could use it immediately. Anthropic also stated that there were no plans to publicly release Mythos in the near future. Does Mythos exist? Yes and no. It only exists on Anthropic's official servers, and in the experiences of these "partners" officially certified by Anthropic. However, this doesn't stop online influencers and AI media from showering it with praise: a grand model that will completely revolutionize all industries, a "final grand model" that crushes all other models... Mythos is the model that's touted as so powerful that 99.999% of the world's population isn't even worthy of using it. However, for ordinary people, its only real significance so far is that it will shape Anthropic and even the entire AI industry, completely reshaping our understanding of the world. But Anthropic isn't the first company to do this. In late 2023, Google released a promotional video showcasing Gemini's multimodal capabilities. In this video, Gemini demonstrates real-time visual understanding of camera footage. It can decipher user hand-drawn doodles, provide real-time commentary while users play games, and even guess which cup the user is playing (a game involving cups and a ball, guessing which cup the ball is hidden in). Its voice is warm and human-like—a marvel in 2023. Google CEO Sundar Pichai also shared the video, which garnered over a million views on YouTube within a day. Tech media outlets wrote extensively about it, praising the advancements in AI that have transcended text-based dialogue, gaining multimodal capabilities and truly entering the real world with profound impact. This carefully constructed perception collapsed just two days later. Under pressure from reporters at Bloomberg and TechCrunch, Google revealed the truth: the so-called real-time dialogue in the video, supposedly performed by Gemini, was actually a response generated by feeding in static images and prompts one by one. Not only was real-time dialogue impossible for the Gemini product at the time, but even the Google DeepMind team itself couldn't implement it internally. The demo itself was fake. After the truth was exposed, Google remained defiant. A company PR representative stated that the video was "an interpretation of the Gemini's possibilities." But we all understand the subtext: like the "dream planting" in *Inception*, the tech industry uses such marketing tactics to plant seeds in users' minds, allowing them to take root, sprout, flourish, and bear fruit. Of course, today, low-latency conversational features and multimodal recognition capabilities have long been implemented by Geminis. But the order of events remains important: Google reaped the fruit before planting the cause. This video was later deleted by Google. But before it was disproven, millions had already watched the video and participated in this performance of reversing cause and effect—how many people still remember that the Gemini team did this? The truth is no longer important. A similar thing happened with the early Sora. In February 2024, OpenAI showcased this video generation model, which was still in training at the time, with incredibly realistic mammoths crossing snowfields and kites soaring in the sky like flocks of birds. Until the end of 2024, the Sora model finally arrived. Users expected to generate stunning and realistic visuals at will, as shown in the teaser video released at the beginning of the year. Instead, they were met with an abstract short video generator that required extensive gacha pulls to barely function. To be fair, OpenAI did clearly state at the beginning of the 2024 video that Sora was still just a research project. However, the "phenomenon" created by that video and the perception it shaped were only barely realized with the official launch of Sora 2 last year. As we all know, the Sora project has now been completely shut down, marking OpenAI's temporary withdrawal from the video generation model race. Sora has completed its life cycle. However, the persistent problem of "phenomena preceding reality" that it demonstrated continues to plague the entire AI industry. No snowflake is innocent. This situation didn't arise out of thin air; industry professionals from different perspectives are involved, each with their own agenda—ultimately forming a conspiracy. AI company product managers, marketing and public relations personnel write press releases, finding the most convincing figures from various benchmark tests. The founders and executives of these companies are extremely adept at creating FOMO (fear of missing out) through speeches, podcasts, and tweets, casting a shadow of fear of unemployment (whether real or not) over the media. Then, media outlets and social media influencers, through articles, tweets, videos, and podcasts, chew, swallow, ruminate, and expel all of the above information. The industry often says that the model is the product. But at a higher level, you'll find that the product is no longer the model itself, but rather the "peripheral system" surrounding it. This includes ever-increasing benchmark scores, and countless AI influencer posts and media reports with titles like "earthquake," "tsunami," and "avalanche," proclaiming the "arrival of the AGI singularity," sending chills down your spine, causing massive bleeding, making you break out in a cold sweat, and even triggering an epileptic seizure. The model is no longer the product; the phenomenon is. Reality no longer constitutes a component of the phenomenon; the phenomenon becomes the new reality.

Further Reading:The AI ​​Hype Bible Released with a Shock by Burying AI

As a typical author for AI media, I would label a model as the "strongest model" after using it for less than a day (most often a few hours is enough); when the GPT version number evolved from 4 to 5, I dared to claim without hesitation that the new model had achieved this or that improvement over GPT-4. "Crushing," "disruptive," and "explosive" have long since become so overinflated in my vocabulary that they're practically worthless. When I close my laptop, I often find myself questioning myself: To what extent can the articles I wrote today withstand the test of time? Have I truly and responsibly described the innovativeness and practical significance of this AI model or product? Ultimately, our daily reporting on this new model, that new product, and this or that technological revolution is no longer simply about how many there are, or even whether they have any innovativeness or practical significance at all. In reality, we often do these reports simply because a product launch is imminent. AI tech media outlets that don't report on these launches, and don't use these terms in their reporting, will fall behind and fail to get recommendations, clicks, reads, or shares. In today's AI marketing landscape, no practitioner can escape the trend. Yes, even APPSO is not immune. Every media outlet, every AI influencer, contributes, large or small, to this cycle of "endless progress." We wrote that Opus 4.7 is the strongest model; then we wrote that Opus 4.7 is "smarter." These viewpoints seem contradictory, yet they are all based on facts—but they are by no means the whole or only fact. Over time, you get used to this internal struggle between your left and right brain. At each point in time, these viewpoints resonate with a certain preference or aesthetic in public opinion, aligning with the current "spirit of the times," so naturally, a succession of media outlets and bloggers will continuously publish and amplify these viewpoints, sparking controversy and gaining traffic. As a reader, you shouldn't expect that after reading this article today, you won't see the kind of articles it criticizes published on APPSO tomorrow. In fact, this satirical article itself becomes the object of its satire. If it weren't for the release of Opus 4.7 and the subsequent controversy, I wouldn't have written this article. Can we finally escape this cycle? It's not entirely hopeless. Models like Claude and GPT have high enough attention and a large enough user base. In the immediate aftermath of release (usually within one or two hours), we can see real user reviews, especially genuine reports from those who directly contradict the marketing rhetoric. It's fair to say that there's still a chance for things to turn around. What impressed me most was the discontinuation of GPT-4o and the release of GPT-5. People had been eagerly anticipating the new major version of GPT, largely thanks to Sam Altman's assessment of the model as having "expert-level intelligence" and his iconic "Death Star" tweet. Then, GPT-5 finally arrived, but it sparked a massive backlash on platforms like X and Reddit. Voices of "intelligence degradation," "inferior to 4O," and "give me back 4O" resounded everywhere. On the prediction market Polymarket, there was a prediction titled "Which company will release the strongest model by the end of August?" OpenAI's percentage in the prediction dropped from over 70% to just over 10% within an hour of GPT-5's release. Unable to withstand the pressure, OpenAI had to bring back the discontinued 4o model. However, this cycle didn't end there: minor versions of GPT-5 were released one after another, with the release and update cycle accelerating from years to months. 4o, after its limited-time return, was eventually discontinued, becoming just another pebble crushed by the relentless march of progress. Considering that Opus 4.7 garnered a lot of attention, and OpenAI hasn't released a new model for quite some time (it feels like an eternity in today's cycle), the rumored "Spud" and the upcoming GPT-6, to be released this year, will each become the "strongest model" in their respective timeframes—this is a foregone conclusion. The previous round of reflection hasn't even ended, and a new cycle has already begun. After you finish reading this article, close the page, and refresh your WeChat Moments, WeChat Official Accounts list, or other social media platforms, you'll see another article about a new model published, possibly DeepSeek V4, Kimi 2.6, Hunyuan 3, or GPT 5.5, or even the truly arriving Mythos. These articles will have the same explosive titles. In the main text, you'll see the scores of the "strongest model" being refreshed again and again. You might think this new model will be the final blow, the last model to end all models… But so what? In Beijing's Zhichun Road, in Hangzhou's Huijin International, in San Francisco's Mission Bay and Market St., an even newer cycle has already begun.

data.title: Nvidia regains its position as the world's most valuable company.
data.description:
Nvidia's market capitalization has firmly established itself above the $5 trillion mark, maintaining its position as the world's most valuable company. On Friday, April 24th, Nvidia's stock price closed up 4.32% at $208.27, surpassing the previous closing record set on October 29th. Year-to-date, Nvidia's stock price has risen by 12%, making it the single largest contributor to the S&P 500's 4.7% gain. Data shows that Nvidia alone accounts for approximately 20% of the index's increase. (Nvidia's stock price has risen by 12% year-to-date.) Just four weeks ago, Nvidia's stock price had fallen 20% from its peak, and this recent surge marks a complete break out of its nearly year-long consolidation range, becoming a landmark event for a new round of AI chip stock market activity in the US. Next week will be a crucial testing period for investors, as Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta will release their quarterly earnings reports. The latest signals regarding AI capital expenditures will influence market sentiment towards Nvidia and the entire chip sector. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index has risen for 18 consecutive days, setting a new historical record. Nvidia's breakthrough occurred against the backdrop of a broad-based rally in the semiconductor sector. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index has closed higher for 18 consecutive trading days, setting a new all-time record, and technical indicators show it to be at its most overbought level ever. This week, Texas Instruments and Intel both announced strong earnings, providing additional support for the sector's performance. As mentioned by Wall Street Insights, Texas Instruments released second-quarter revenue and profit guidance, both exceeding Wall Street expectations. Its stock price surged approximately 20% on Thursday, marking its best single-day performance since the bursting of the dot-com bubble, before closing slightly lower by 1.8% on Friday. After the US stock market closed on Thursday, Intel's stock price surged as much as 28% on Friday following the release of strong earnings and optimistic outlook guidance. This was the largest single-day gain since "Black Monday" in 1987. The stock ultimately closed up about 21%, surpassing its previous all-time high set at the peak of the dot-com bubble. Market strategists generally attribute this rebound to the continued surge in investment in AI infrastructure. Paul Nolte, market strategist at Murphy & Sylvest Wealth Management, stated: "The level of investment in AI is staggering, and we haven't seen any signs of a slowdown yet. Against this backdrop, it's not surprising that Nvidia has become a star performer in the tech stock comeback rally." However, he also pointed out that the market still needs more data to confirm this: "I'm waiting for the earnings reports to get a clearer picture of the capital expenditure outlook, but even so, I think there are still plenty of reasons to be optimistic about Nvidia's prospects."

Microsoft and other tech giants' earnings reports will be key variables next week

Besides the rising interest in AI infrastructure, geopolitical influences are also driving investors to seek safe haven in the technology sector, which has solid earnings growth support.However, whether the current market optimism is sustainable largely depends on the earnings reports of tech giants to be released next week.

But whether the current market optimism is sustainable largely depends on the earnings reports of tech giants to be released next week.

Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta, as the four largest capital spenders in the AI ​​infrastructure sector, will provide important guidance for the medium-term trend of the chip sector through their quarterly results and management's outlook on future investments. Analysts believe that if these companies maintain or increase their AI infrastructure spending plans, it will directly strengthen market expectations for increased demand for Nvidia. Conversely, any signs of slowdown could put pressure on current valuations.

data.title: Vertically integrated capital aggregator: How Web3 builds an impenetrable moat
data.description:
Author: @Decentralisedco; Translation: AididiaoJP, Foresight News

Today's story is about what makes protocols antifragile and how tokens are used as leverage to move ecosystems. In the weeks following the hack, we delved into the Hyperliquid ecosystem. We quickly discovered that protocols in this industry are building formidable moats through vertical integration.

This article explores how Web3 leaders maintain their moats.

I frequently use terms like "supply-side aggregator," "demand-side aggregator," and "vertically integrated capital ecosystem" in this article. For ease of understanding:

  • Supply-side aggregators refer to aggregating differentiated market participants to offer commoditized products (e.g., Uber).

    Supply-side aggregators are those that bring together differentiated market participants to offer commoditized products (e.g., Uber).

    Demand-side aggregators refer to groups of externally similar companies that expand their product offerings (e.g., Amazon). Vertically integrated capital aggregators refer to financial entities that integrate multiple parts of an ecosystem, offering various products to users in a single venue. Blockchain is the monetary track. The value of a protocol lies in its economic output. Composability and real-time verifiability enable vertical integration in blockchain-native businesses. Tokens allow participants in the stack to be incentivized through a shared medium. Teams that intentionally capture value in every part of the stack possess a moat. Vertical integration helps accelerate the flow of capital within the ecosystem. If done well, it becomes a revenue stream. Uber aggregates passengers across the city, positioning itself as a demand aggregator, and so does Swiggy. You might think there's not much difference between an ordinary passenger and a hungry person; they can be commodified. Therefore, as a platform, Uber can take a 30% cut from every aggregated "goods" (i.e., human users). Restaurants hate this, and drivers hate it too. They all try to bypass the platform by having users pay directly in cash. Passengers (or rather, I) understand that the platform's value lies in its ability to implement a reputation system, and cash transactions are not an option today. You see something similar with Jupiter on Solana. Its early influence came from its ability to route orders across exchanges and provide users with the best prices. Unlike restaurants and drivers, Jupiter aggregated exchange venues for my WIF buy orders. However, Substack and Spotify operate on different parallel tracks. Spotify pays up to 70% of its revenue to record labels. Last year, it paid $13.75 billion of its $20.22 billion revenue to rights holders. For every $1 in revenue generated, only $0.04 ultimately reaches the artist. Substack takes only 10% of what I pay to the newsletter. It neither commodifies its readers nor its authors. It simply positions itself as a tool. Perhaps this is intentional—if it had pricing power, authors on the platform might simply leave. Spotify, with its partnerships with numerous record labels, is, to some extent, a loose supply-side aggregator. Substack, on the other hand, positions itself as a demand-side aggregator without pricing power, relying on its reader base. These apps each aggregate different aspects of the market. But their ability to accumulate capital (or pricing power) depends on how tightly they are integrated into the ecosystem. We will soon see another version. Web2 native aggregators leverage the massive user base driven by two forces: Moore's Law has drastically reduced the cost of smartphones. India alone has 800 million smartphone users. Today, approximately 5.5 billion people are online globally. Internet costs are decreasing and bandwidth costs are rapidly declining. In contrast, cryptocurrency as an economy has a much smaller TAM (Trading Average Transaction Value). According to the best estimates, approximately 560 million users have interacted with cryptocurrencies. Last month, the number of active DeFi wallets was around 10 million. These are fundamentally different economies with very different sets of rules. One is attention-driven, the other is driven by funds flowing on-chain within wallets. We often confuse the behavior of the attention economy with that of the transaction economy. For example, user behavior in prediction markets is not the same as user behavior on Instagram. Re-examining Aggregation Three years ago, when I first wrote about aggregators, I thought blockchain reduced the cost of verification and trust. The initial promise of the internet was access; you could sit in New York in your pajamas and buy Temu products from Shenzhen. At the time, I believed that blockchain would enable real-time verification and settlement of transactions with suppliers at extremely low cost. Or rather, I predicted this industry development. In 2022, I wrote: "We believe that blockchain will spawn a completely new market capable of instantly verifying on-chain events. This will drastically reduce the cost of large-scale verification of intellectual property, thereby creating new business models." In the years that followed, new markets did indeed emerge. NFT trading volume has reached approximately $100 billion since then. Perpetual contract liquidations have reached approximately $14.6 trillion. Decentralized exchange liquidations have reached approximately $10.8 trillion. The understanding that technology would be used to settle transactions globally with anonymous counterparties is correct. However, it must also be noted that OpenSea's trading volume has declined from approximately $5 billion then to approximately $70 million this month. Markets emerge, evolve, and then disappear. Just like many things in life. In the process, they leave us with the fuel for reflection. Sid believes that speculation in cryptocurrencies is a characteristic, not a mistake. All new markets are ambiguous in their early stages; participants don't know what they're buying or why something has value. Novelty is built into the price. When rationality returns and valuations become effective, the bubble becomes a memory. NFTs and DeFi have both experienced such frenzied cycles. These bubbles are crucial for stress testing and validating the monetary tracks that ultimately laid the foundation for markets like Hyperliquid. This reminds me of my experiences in the crypto space over the past few weeks. In the Drift and Kelp incidents, approximately $578 million was hacked in the past three weeks. In the past twelve months, approximately $1.7 billion has been stolen from DeFi and crypto protocols tracked by DefiLlama. Meanwhile, total DeFi protocol revenue was approximately $3.42 billion during the same period. In other words, for every $1 earned in DeFi revenue over the past year, about $0.50 was lost due to hacking. At the same time, we've seen more and more apps being released, and the software itself has become a commodity. The number of apps submitted to the App Store this quarter increased by 84% compared to the same period last year. We're seeing two forces at work: more software competing for the attention of fewer users, and a few platforms dominating the majority of revenue generated in the crypto space. Now we realize that for every $1 this industry earns, $0.50 is lost. The turnaround is steep—but keep thinking with me. If we break down the $3.5 billion in revenue generated by decentralized channels, you'll quickly see a pattern. Approximately 40% comes from derivatives platforms, with Hyperliquid alone contributing about $902 million. The second largest category is decentralized exchanges, with Uniswap, the leader, generating approximately $927 million in fees. Lending platforms like MakerDAO ranked third, totaling approximately $500 million in revenue. A common characteristic of these is that they are all capital-intensive businesses. Unlike products that can be built with a few lines of code on a Saturday afternoon, they require the coordination of patient capital willing to accept the risks of these platforms. This is where you realize the main difference between Web2 aggregators and Web3 native aggregators. Because blockchains are primarily tools for transferring funds and verifying whether transactions follow a set of rules set by developers—they only become valuable when they can perform capital-intensive operations. Perpetual contract exchanges can repeatedly inject large sums of money into productive uses within the same day. Lending platforms, on the other hand, take a small cut of the substantial revenue generated. For example, Aave earned approximately $123 million from the $920 million in revenue it generated over the past year. However, such aggregators can only dominate the market if they possess three key elements simultaneously: Supply side (liquidity), Demand side (users), and Distribution. Hyperliquid is a unique beast in this regard. It has paid nearly $100 million in code fees to its builders, but the vast majority of its revenue comes from its own native frontend. It retains its best users while expanding the surface area of ​​interaction between new users and the protocol. But what's the logic behind this? One theory is that in Web3, distribution is a toll. Large protocols tend to own and retain their best users. This becomes clear when you notice the revenue generated by decentralized exchanges compared to on-chain aggregators that route orders. On Ethereum, aggregators account for 36% of all DEX trading volume. On Solana, this percentage can be as low as around 7% depending on the month. Kyber, 1inch, CoW, and ParaSwap have collectively generated only about $112 million in fees since their launch. Uniswap, as an independent exchange that dominates the majority of trading volume, has generated approximately $5.5 billion in fees over its lifetime. You can see a similar phenomenon on Hyperliquid. Builder code collectively accounts for only about 6% of Hyperliquid's cumulative $1.1 billion in revenue. MetaMask's deep integration on Ethereum earned $184 million in fees through swaps last year. Phantom generated about $180 million, but this is only a small fraction of its massive ecosystem. These products only work when built on a single protocol with liquidity and economic activity. These products attract and retain users precisely because they have deep liquidity. From this perspective—capital in cryptocurrency is no longer a commoditized product. It is the most essential component. Vertical integration of capital provides participants with more reasons to remain within the ecosystem. In such a system, capital brings more liquidity because it can be put into productive use. Capital is not the moat; it is the result of vertical integration. Vertical integration is the moat; capital is merely a byproduct. It's important to note that this model only works when there is no incentive to park capital. Don't believe it? Look at any pre-launch protocol with an airdrop plan, or look at the countless L2s striving to generate value. Any business engaged in capital aggregation is, to some extent, a target for hackers. Drift was targeted because it held approximately $570 million in TVL. KelpDAO was targeted because it held approximately $1.6 billion in re-stakinged ETH. Hyperliquid holds approximately $2 billion in user deposits on its bridges, making it one of the most valuable targets in the space. You can see similar situations with Ronin ($625 million) and Nomad ($190 million). Because blockchain-native businesses require significant capital to generate value, we face a dynamic where, to win, we must remain vulnerable until security mechanisms and mechanisms to freeze fund flows are in place. Even if you have capital, a large TVL (Total Value Limit) itself doesn't guarantee success. In an economy, idle or underutilized capital can become a liability when hacked. This is why protocols try to differentiate themselves through the economic output they can generate, starting with niche use cases. CHIP (the company behind USDAI) has disbursed approximately $100 million in loans this quarter and has $1.5 billion in pipelines underway, these higher-risk tokens will generate approximately 16% APY this year. The APY of Maple's highest-risk pools is approximately 15–20%, comparable to or higher than the current 12.6% APY of Aave's USDC pools. It aggregates borrowers capable of generating economic output using protocol liquidity. Naturally, Hyperliquid is a prime example of a supply-side aggregator that can channel capital in meaningful ways. Over the past year, Hyperliquid generated approximately $942 million in revenue, with an average TVL of approximately $3.5 billion. Roughly speaking, every $1 of capital held on the protocol turns over approximately 285 times per year, generating approximately $0.30 in fees per $1 of TVL. This contrasts with Aave's approximately $0.05 in fees per $1 of TVL in its lending market. In a market where consumer preferences are not yet fixed and investor loyalty is low, capital flows to where the best results are achieved. When considering hacking risks, investors demand a risk premium. Currently, perpetual contract exchanges are the only place where idle capital can be repeatedly used on-chain and generate fees. I initially thought Hyperliquid was just a supply-side aggregator that provided capital to users willing to trade on-chain. That's always been my argument. And it's true. But this argument falls apart when you consider how it uses tokens to incentivize vertical integration. But before we continue, let me explain how a vertically integrated ecosystem works. Ticketmaster handles 70% of major live shows in the US. It takes 30% of your ticket to Justin Bieber's Coachella concert because it owns the venue, promotes the tour, ensures you can buy merchandise at the concert, and coordinates with sponsors. That 30% you pay is 15 times what Stripe handles for online ticketing. But you're willing to pay that premium because Ticketmaster has achieved vertical integration on the value chain. You have a market illusion: artists, venues, and fans are all participants, but no one can object to Ticketmaster's commission. The same is true for Apple's App Store. Apple manages the curation store, collects fees, ensures devices work properly, and brings in millions of users accustomed to the Apple Pay "ding"—even if you've subscribed to yet another app you'll never use. Vertical Integration in Cryptocurrency Protocols are beginning to implement the same logic, albeit slowly. In Web3, without vertical integration making it easier for capital providers to collaborate, they can be considered commodities. Users won't develop loyalty until the ecosystem is integrated into a cumulative experience that can't be replicated elsewhere. For Maple, this integration requires years of experience dealing with hedge funds and market makers. For Centrifuge, the integration included securing nearly $1 billion from Grove for Janus Henderson's JAAA bond issuance. Instead of capturing loose, abstract parts of the economy, they vertically integrated to provide better products for end users. By doing so, they created a moat that is difficult to replicate overnight. Maple's years of underwriting experience, or Centrifuge's moat as a trusted capital coordinator, is a moat in a world where capital and relationships are the only things that are difficult to replicate. Companies that engage in vertical integration may routinely outsource certain parts of their stack to third parties. Part of the reason for this is that there is no significant economic benefit to doing so. Maple owning custody or MetaMask issuing its own cards may not generate substantial profits compared to the capital they generate in swap and credit underwriting. However, when a business grows exponentially, owning the entire stack is where you build a competitive advantage. This is also part of the reason for mergers and acquisitions in the industry. When a company achieves vertical integration, you're not competing against a single product. The battle is against the overall experience users get within it. On Hyperliquid, once HIP-4 launched, users could deposit for free (through Native Markets), participate in prediction market positions, and trade those positions as collateral on its perpetual products. Its risk engine made this possible. And it's worth noting that even in today's traditional finance, this would be impossible without merchant banks. Hyperliquid has users, deposit channels, a risk engine, a trading interface, liquidity, and token issuance rights. For a new business to compete with this means fighting on six different fronts simultaneously. For a new application launching, integrating into a small portion of these is far better than building on a new protocol like Monad, which has only $2.6 billion in cumulative derivatives trading volume (spread across its five perpetual protocols). Integrated ecosystems like Hyperliquid attract developers, more integration, headlines, and happy token holders. Exchanges have also seen this shift. Coinbase acquired Deribit, has a custody business, co-issues USDC with Circle and earns revenue from reserves, has a massive wallet infrastructure, and deposit gateways in over 100 countries. It has also launched its own chain in pursuit of a vertically integrated experience. Admittedly, Coinbase may be too early in pursuing retail users who clearly don't want to "mint" content on the blockchain or use Farcaster. Coinbase's integration exists in a loose form, but is hidden behind layers of bureaucracy, regulatory hurdles, and internal priorities. This is perhaps the key difference between open and closed integration systems. As an exchange with a market capitalization of approximately $60 billion, Coinbase has little incentive to pursue peripheral developers. In contrast, Hyperliquid benefits by developing its core channels into the best trading venues while simultaneously creating an ecosystem and generating value for the underlying tokens. In this context, the token is part of the integration, as it is the shared substrate that sustains and values ​​these integrations. This is why the industry confuses tokenization protocols with tokenization businesses. Tokenization protocols presuppose that third-party developers can easily build on them. It incentivizes people to drive value (downward) to tokens—usually in the form of buybacks from the market. Companies like Robinhood and Coinbase are powerful economic players, but they cannot replicate Hyperliquid's core owners—the operator network. Protocol airdrops ensure that those who own it are individuals who have made economic contributions to it. They possess enough tokens to drive value towards it. Hyperliquid is committed to this cause by dedicating 99% of its revenue to buying back tokens from the market. Imagine a publicly traded company using 100% of its revenue to buy back its employees' ESOPs. We might see increased acceptance of capitalism. This is why industries are evolving—whether we like it culturally or not. Solana focuses on immutability, Ethereum on censorship resistance and open source, but you'll see industries adapting their ideologies to business realities. Hyperliquid, while a beautiful garden, is a walled garden. To my knowledge, its source code is not open source. How its risk engine works is unverifiable. Maple's risk underwriting parameters are not public. As a lender, I probably don't even know who underwrites the loans on USDAI. Negotiating with Chaos An economy cannot be built if $0.50 of every $1 of revenue generated is lost to hacking. If founders are held accountable for hundreds of millions of dollars in TVL sitting on their products, they will flock to AI. Whenever a hack occurs, we rush to hope that stablecoins will be frozen. This, in turn, often means centralization. Vertically integrated stacks ultimately need to abandon complete decentralization for economic progress. This is not a new story on the internet. In the late 1990s, there was a grand dream of an open internet that allowed free speech without consequences. Nazi memorabilia was auctioned on Yahoo until a French court intervened in 2000. Tim Wu explores this theme in depth in *Who Controls the Internet?*. The story of the internet, or indeed all human commercial networks, is about complete decentralization giving way to a milder version, relinquishing some control for economic interaction. We accept a diluted version of the original vision so that businesses can scale, because without dilution, chaos will ensue. This immense expansion of energy is reflected in how we describe those eras: the "Wild West," the internet "bubble." Perhaps cryptocurrencies are also experiencing a similar expansion and energy convergence. I explored these topics in depth last year in the following article. What does this mean for founders? Look at the data from MetaMask and Phantom. These businesses earn more money than most L2 exchanges because they are downstream in an ecosystem with huge economic output. Building bridges and exchanges where there is no liquidity and users is no longer a viable business model. Especially when it comes with the pain of hacking. You should build where there is liquidity and users today. It may not be possible to imitate a vertically integrated product overnight, but you can build on top of it. Operating systems have also gone through a similar pattern. When BlackBerry declined and iOS became dominant, developers had to choose where to build. We are seeing a similar situation in the crypto space. Only this time, capital incentives may keep developers blind for a longer period of time. Platforms and protocols on the internet are very similar. We may not like the rules, but they keep things functioning. In the era of vertical integrators, we may increasingly agree on certain rules so that our money stays with us and the economies we invest significant time in can continue to scale. The trend points in this direction. Stablecoins, RWAs, perpetual contract exchanges with closed-loop risk engines, lending platforms with unknown risk underwriters, and off-chain RFQ products like Derive all point to the same trend. That is, vertically integrated capital aggregators willing to abandon the dream of complete decentralization for the sake of progress.

data.title: The United States has frozen $344 million in Iranian cryptocurrency.
data.description: The US has taken action against Iran again. According to reports from CCTV News and other media outlets, on April 24th local time, the US Treasury Department's Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC), frequently mentioned in international financial sanctions news, released a new round of sanctions against Iran and simultaneously updated its "Specially Designated Nationals List." Notably, this time the US is not only targeting institutions, accounts, and transaction networks within the traditional financial system, but also including multiple cryptocurrency wallet addresses linked to Iran in the sanctions, involving approximately $344 million in frozen cryptocurrency assets. src="https://mmbiz.qpic.cn/mmbiz_jpg/iaEv22TKgUOIib9BUjFNhxHbwGspichdQtvzonHDxsvse13fxI9U62dlv1EK03LUIqKlNyDd3QSeAup cficLlu0cwklIUxga4ibtSrcwTkJvQ9W4/640?wx_fmt=jpeg&tp=wxpic&wxfrom=5&wx_lazy=1&watermark=1#imgIndex=0" class="" _="" alt="image" data-before-load-time="1777088893809" data-report-img-idx="0" Reuters also reported that US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent stated on social media that the US Treasury Department is sanctioning multiple wallets linked to Iran and has frozen $344 million in cryptocurrency assets. More crucial information comes from Tether's own announcement. According to Tether, it cooperated with OFAC and US law enforcement agencies to freeze over $344 million in USDT from two addresses. The announcement also mentioned that these addresses were identified after US law enforcement agencies provided relevant information, and the freezing action was to prevent further transfer of funds. Therefore, simply stopping at "the US freezes Iranian cryptocurrency assets" is insufficient.
